subject: History
trap_factor: Cripps Mission (1942) also proposed a federal union and is often confused with Cabinet Mission; both are post-war proposals. The phrase 'still wanted a united India' and 'May' proposals are key Cabinet Mission identifiers.
elimination_hint: Simon Commission had no federation plan; Gandhi-Irwin Pact was about civil disobedience; Cripps Mission (1942) proposed dominion status after the war — Cabinet Mission (1946, May) is the only one with Federal Union of British provinces.
factual_hooks: Cabinet Mission 1946 — proposed Federal Union with three tiers
Cabinet Mission submitted its plan in May 1946
It was the last British attempt to keep India united
contrast_with: Cripps Mission — proposed dominion status, not full Federal Union plan

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Option A (disease-free conditions) is plausible as labs are sterile; option B (conserve endangered) sounds environmentally correct — both are distractors.
elimination_hint: Phytotron = 'phyto' (plant) + 'tron' (controlled chamber) — it is a controlled environment facility for plant research; elimination via root words.
factual_hooks: Phytotron: facility to grow plants under controlled conditions of light, temperature, humidity
Used in plant physiology research
contrast_with: Herbarium — preserved plant specimens, not live growth

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Growth hormone is often associated with gonads (sex-related growth), and Insulin is confused with adrenal gland secretions; carefully distinguishing pancreas→insulin is key.
elimination_hint: Anchor: Pancreas→Insulin (most well-known); then Adrenal→Cortisone; Gonads→Progesterone (sex hormones); Pituitary→Growth hormone. Only option C matches all four correctly.
factual_hooks: Gonads secrete sex hormones (progesterone/estrogen/testosterone)
Pituitary is master gland — secretes growth hormone
Pancreas→Insulin; Adrenal cortex→Cortisone
contrast_with: Exocrine glands — secrete via ducts (e.g. sweat glands) vs endocrine — ductless

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Students often think the gene comes from an insect since it targets insects; the Bt gene comes from Bacillus thuringiensis (bacterium), not from the insect itself.
elimination_hint: Bt cotton = Bacillus thuringiensis gene inserted; 'Bt' stands for a bacterium. The cry gene from this bacterium produces a protein toxic to insects.
factual_hooks: Bt cotton contains gene from Bacillus thuringiensis (bacterium)
Cry protein produced is toxic to bollworm
First GM crop commercially approved in India (2002)
contrast_with: Hybridization — cross-breeding vs genetic engineering — gene insertion
contemporary_relevance: Ongoing debate on GM mustard and other GM crops in India relevant to biotech policy.

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Interferon and general antibodies are also biotech products; hybridoma specifically produces monoclonal (single-clone specific) antibodies, not polyclonal antibodies.
elimination_hint: Hybridoma = hybrid + oma (tumor cell); fusing B-lymphocyte with myeloma (cancer) cell creates immortal cell producing one specific antibody — monoclonal antibodies.
factual_hooks: Hybridoma = B lymphocyte fused with myeloma cell
Produces monoclonal antibodies (MAbs) — highly specific
Used in diagnostics (ELISA), cancer therapy, and pregnancy tests
contrast_with: Polyclonal antibodies — from multiple B-cell clones vs monoclonal — from single clone
contemporary_relevance: Monoclonal antibodies used in COVID-19 therapies and cancer treatment.

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Hard water is commonly thought unsafe for drinking (statement I), but it is actually not harmful to health — it is unsuitable for washing and boilers only. Statement IV (irrigation) is also misleading.
elimination_hint: Hard water causes scale in boilers (III) and curdles soap (II); it is NOT harmful for drinking or crops. Only II and III are correct — option B.
factual_hooks: Hard water contains Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺ ions
Reacts with soap to form scum (insoluble salts)
Causes scale deposition in boilers — safety hazard
contrast_with: Soft water — lathers easily, safe for boilers

subject: Polity
trap_factor: Interpellation sounds procedurally relevant (questions in parliament); decorum refers to general order. 'Yielding the floor' is a specific parliamentary term not commonly known.
elimination_hint: Eliminate: decorum=general conduct, crossing the floor=changing party, interpellation=formal question process. Yielding the floor=giving up speaking time to another member.
factual_hooks: Yielding the floor: speaker allowing another member to speak in their place
Parliamentary procedure term used in Westminster system
contrast_with: Interpellation — formal questioning of minister vs yielding the floor — procedural speaking rights

subject: History
trap_factor: Gandhi is associated with promoting Hindi; Nehru promoted scientific temperament not script changes; Azad is associated with Urdu. Bose's presidential address on Roman script is an obscure fact.
elimination_hint: Subhas Chandra Bose as Congress President (1938, Haripura session) made this proposal — a counter-intuitive fact. Eliminate Gandhi (promoted Devanagari) and Nehru (no such advocacy).
factual_hooks: Subhas Chandra Bose advocated Roman script for Hindi in his Congress presidential address
He was Congress President at Haripura session (1938)
contrast_with: Gandhi — promoted Hindi in Devanagari as national language

subject: History
trap_factor: Punjab is the most obvious choice given partition violence there; Assam had its own autonomy issues. Bengal's united independent existence plan (Sarat Bose-Suhrawardy plan) is less known.
elimination_hint: The Bengal Pact for united independent Bengal was proposed by Sarat Chandra Bose and Huseyn Shaheed Suhrawardy in 1947 — unique to Bengal; Punjab was divided without such a joint plan.
factual_hooks: Bengal had a plan for united independent sovereign state (1947) — Bose-Suhrawardy plan
Plan was rejected by Congress and Muslim League leadership
Punjab was partitioned without any such united state proposal
contrast_with: Punjab — partitioned along religious lines with no united state proposal

subject: Polity
trap_factor: The order of precedence places CJI above CEC and Cabinet Ministers above Cabinet Secretary; students mix up positions of CEC and CJI relative to Cabinet Ministers.
elimination_hint: Correct order: Cabinet Ministers (III) > Chief Justice (IV) > Chief Election Commissioner (II) > Cabinet Secretary (I). Option C matches this sequence.
factual_hooks: Order of Precedence: Cabinet Ministers > CJI > CEC > Cabinet Secretary
Order of Precedence is governed by the Warrant of Precedence
contrast_with: Order of precedence vs seniority within judiciary

subject: Polity
trap_factor: Options B and D confuse Finance Commission with Ministry of Finance/Planning Commission roles; option C (advise President) is partially true but not the primary function.
elimination_hint: Finance Commission under Article 280 is primarily tasked with recommending distribution of tax revenues between Centre and States — vertical and horizontal devolution.
factual_hooks: Finance Commission: Article 280 of Constitution
Recommends vertical devolution (Centre-State) and horizontal devolution (inter-state)
Also recommends grants-in-aid to states
contrast_with: Planning Commission (now NITI Aayog) — policy body vs Finance Commission — constitutional revenue-sharing body
contemporary_relevance: 16th Finance Commission recommendations being awaited for 2026-31 period.

subject: History
trap_factor: Statement III is the trap — A.O. Hume was a founder of INC but never its President; the first British President was George Yule (1888), not Hume. Statement I is also wrong — Sarojini Naidu was the first Indian woman, not the first woman.
elimination_hint: Eliminate I (Hume founded but never presided) and III; C.R. Das was indeed in prison (Gaya Congress 1922) and Alfred Webb presided in 1894 — so II and IV are correct. Answer: B.
factual_hooks: C.R. Das (Deshbandhu) presided INC from prison — Gaya session 1922
Alfred Webb — British, INC President 1894
A.O. Hume founded INC (1885) but never served as President
related_concepts: George Yule — first British INC President
contrast_with: AO Hume — founder but not President vs George Yule — first British President of INC

subject: History
trap_factor: Bhimpalasi and Bhopali are both early morning-associated ragas in popular perception; Todi is the specifically designated pre-sunrise/early morning classical raga.
elimination_hint: Indian classical music: Ragas are time-specific. Todi is the morning raga (pre-noon); Darbari is night raga; Bhimpalasi is afternoon. Eliminate by time-association.
factual_hooks: Raga Todi is sung in early morning (before noon)
Indian classical music associates specific ragas with specific times (prahar)
contrast_with: Darbari — night raga vs Todi — morning raga

subject: History
trap_factor: Students confuse Maurya with Nanda — Chandragupta Maurya defeated the Nandas after Alexander's invasion; Alexander's invasion (327-325 BCE) predates Mauryan Empire (321 BCE).
elimination_hint: Alexander invaded around 326 BCE; the Nanda dynasty ruled North India at that time. Chandragupta Maurya overthrew the Nandas only after Alexander departed.
factual_hooks: Nanda dynasty ruled at time of Alexander's invasion (326 BCE)
Chandragupta Maurya overthrew Nandas c.321 BCE to establish Mauryan Empire
Alexander retreated due to his army's refusal to advance further east
contrast_with: Mauryan Empire — post-Alexander vs Nanda Empire — contemporary with Alexander

subject: Governance
trap_factor: This is an obscure 1997 scheme detail; PM's Integrated Urban Poverty Eradication Programme (PMIUPEP) was not subsumed — it is the one NOT included in SJSRY.
elimination_hint: Swarna Jayanti Shahari Rozgar Yojana (SJSRY) merged NRY, UBSP, and PMRY — three schemes. PMIUPEP was kept separate. Eliminate by process of elimination after knowing three included schemes.
factual_hooks: SJSRY (1997) merged: Nehru Rozgar Yojana, Urban Basic Services Programme, and Prime Minister's Rozgar Yojana
PM's Integrated Urban Poverty Eradication Programme was NOT merged
contrast_with: SJSRY — urban vs SGSY — rural livelihood scheme

subject: History
trap_factor: Statement II is the trap — Chakiarkoothu was traditionally off-limits to higher caste Hindus, not lower caste; it was exclusively for Chakiar caste and low-caste audience separation is reversed.
elimination_hint: Chakiarkoothu: performed by Chakiars (I correct), Mizhavu is the instrument (III correct), Koothambalam is theatre (IV correct). Statement II is incorrect — higher castes could watch. Answer: I, III, IV = A.
factual_hooks: Chakiarkoothu: Kerala classical performance by Chakiar caste
Mizhavu: copper drum instrument used as accompaniment
Koothambalam: traditional temple theatre for Chakiarkoothu
contrast_with: Koodiyattam — involves multiple castes vs Chakiarkoothu — exclusively Chakiar caste

subject: CSAT
trap_factor: Sign errors when substituting X=-2 into X³ are common; X³ = (-2)³ = -8, not +8.
elimination_hint: Substitute X=-2: (-8) - (4) - (-2) - 1 = -8 - 4 + 2 - 1 = -11. Careful with signs at each step.
factual_hooks: Substitution method for polynomial evaluation

subject: CSAT
trap_factor: Students may calculate total 10×10=100 sq cm and subtract wrongly; the shaded portion needs careful step-pattern area calculation.
elimination_hint: For a cross/step shape cut from a 10cm square, compute the shaded area by identifying number of unit squares or using the complement method (total - unshaded).
factual_hooks: Area calculation for irregular shapes: use complement or direct counting
Equal-length right-angle segments form a specific pattern

subject: CSAT
trap_factor: With 3 males and 3 females and only one married couple, it's easy to misassign genders; the clue that B is son of A and A and E are sons of F helps establish the male line.
elimination_hint: A, E = sons of F (males). B = son of A (male). D = mother of one boy, one girl = female. One married couple among adults = A married to D. F is likely female (grandmother). Answer: B.
factual_hooks: Blood relation puzzles: draw family tree, establish gender first
One married couple constraint limits possibilities significantly

subject: CSAT
trap_factor: Students often confuse 'failed in at least two subjects' with 'failed in exactly two subjects'; must include those who failed in all three as well.
elimination_hint: Sum regions of Venn diagram where two or more circles overlap; divide by 500 and multiply by 100 for percentage. 'At least two' = exactly two + all three.
factual_hooks: Venn diagram: 'at least two' = exactly two + exactly three (all)
Percentage = (count/total) × 100
contrast_with: At least two vs exactly two — critical distinction in Venn problems

subject: CSAT
trap_factor: The unit conversion from cm to metres is critical; painting cost per sq metre but dimensions in cm leads to errors if conversion is skipped.
elimination_hint: Circumference = 100cm = 1m (width of sheet becomes circumference), height = 50cm = 0.5m. Lateral surface area = 1×0.5 = 0.5 m². Cost = 0.5 × 50 = Rs.25.
factual_hooks: Rolling a rectangle into cylinder: length = circumference, width = height
Lateral surface area of cylinder = 2πr × h = circumference × height

subject: CSAT
trap_factor: Students add 65.8 + 59.2 and forget to subtract 100 (since total is 100%, not 125%). The overlap (both subjects) = sum of percentages − 100.
elimination_hint: Using inclusion-exclusion: Both = Physics% + Maths% − 100% = 65.8 + 59.2 − 100 = 25%. 25% of 2000 = 500.
factual_hooks: Inclusion-exclusion principle: |A∪B| = |A| + |B| − |A∩B|
When all candidates take at least one, |A∪B| = 100%

subject: Geography
trap_factor: Sao Paulo is Brazil's largest city and often confused as the coffee port; Santos is the actual port city through which coffee is exported.
elimination_hint: Santos is the port city of Sao Paulo state, Brazil — historically the world's largest coffee export port. Sao Paulo is inland; Santos is on the coast.
factual_hooks: Santos, Brazil = 'Coffee port of the world'
Sao Paulo state is the world's largest coffee producing region
Santos handles ~40% of Brazil's exports
contrast_with: Sao Paulo — city vs Santos — port for coffee export

subject: Environment
trap_factor: Matching industrial units to feed materials requires specific knowledge of which plant uses which biomass; confusing distillery waste with black liquor or slaughter-house waste is common.
elimination_hint: Key anchors: Paper mills use black liquor (lignin byproduct); Sugar mills generate distillery spent wash; Slaughter-house = animal waste. Rice husk → Gowthami (oil/rice state).
factual_hooks: Black liquor: byproduct of paper/pulp industry used for energy
Distillery spent wash: liquid waste from alcohol distillation used in biogas
contrast_with: Black liquor (paper industry) vs distillery spent wash (sugar industry)

subject: History
trap_factor: Akbar's death in 1605 and capture of Khandesh in 1601 are close dates; the map extent at 1601 (Khandesh capture) vs 1605 (death) differs in the Deccan region.
elimination_hint: Akbar captured Khandesh (Asirgarh) in 1601; the empire at that point excludes territories added between 1601-1605. Aurangzeb's empire was much larger. Focus on Deccan extent.
factual_hooks: Akbar captured Khandesh/Asirgarh fort in 1601 — last major conquest
Mughal empire's maximum extent under Akbar excludes South Deccan
contrast_with: Akbar's empire (1601) vs Aurangzeb's empire (1707) — South India extent differs

subject: History
trap_factor: Qutab Minar construction started ~1193 but completed later; students may confuse Firoz Tughlaq's death (1388) with late Sultanate period. Portuguese arrival (1498) is often placed incorrectly.
elimination_hint: Chronological order: Qutab Minar construction (~1193) → Firoz Tughlaq death (1388) → Portuguese arrival (1498) → Krishna Deva Raya reign (1509-1529). Correct: II, IV, III, I = Answer A.
factual_hooks: Qutab Minar: begun by Qutb-ud-din Aibak c.1193 CE
Firoz Shah Tughlaq died 1388 CE
Portuguese (Vasco da Gama) arrived in India 1498 CE
Krishna Deva Raya reigned 1509-1529 CE

subject: History
trap_factor: This is a map-based question about IC-814 hijacking (1999) requiring knowledge of actual airport sequence: Amritsar → Lahore → Dubai → Kandahar — very event-specific.
elimination_hint: IC-814 hijacking route: Kathmandu (origin) → Amritsar → Lahore → Dubai → Kandahar (final). Match map labels to these cities in sequence.
factual_hooks: IC-814 hijacking 1999: Kathmandu → Amritsar → Lahore → Dubai → Kandahar
Ended with release of 3 militants including Masood Azhar

subject: Geography
trap_factor: Statement I is the trap — Roaring Forties blow only in Southern Hemisphere (uninterrupted by land masses), not in Northern Hemisphere where continents block them.
elimination_hint: Eliminate I (they blow only in Southern Hemisphere due to absence of land barriers). Statements II, III (NW to East in SH), IV are all correct. Answer: B.
factual_hooks: Roaring Forties: westerly winds between 40°-50° South latitude
Blow uninterrupted in Southern Hemisphere — no land to break them
Direction: west to east (NW to E) in Southern Hemisphere
contrast_with: Northern Hemisphere westerlies — interrupted by continents vs Southern Hemisphere Roaring Forties — uninterrupted

subject: Polity
trap_factor: Students may think party membership or principal's permission are required; the only legal requirement is being a registered voter (name in voters' list) in the constituency.
elimination_hint: Electoral law: A candidate must be a registered voter in the constituency (or anywhere for some bodies). No party membership or college permission required. Answer: C.
factual_hooks: To contest elections, name must be in voters' list — basic eligibility
No party membership required to contest local body elections in India
contrast_with: Eligibility to vote vs eligibility to contest — different thresholds

subject: Geography
trap_factor: Yugoslavia and Bosnia are often assumed to be in the center-east; Slovenia is the westernmost, Croatia is west-center, Bosnia is central, Yugoslavia (Serbia) is east.
elimination_hint: West to East: Slovenia (westernmost) → Croatia → Bosnia → Yugoslavia/Serbia. So East to West reversed: Yugoslavia, Bosnia, Croatia, Slovenia = IV, I, II, III. Answer B.
factual_hooks: Former Yugoslavia breakup: Slovenia and Croatia declared independence 1991
Geographic sequence E to W: Yugoslavia → Bosnia → Croatia → Slovenia

subject: History
trap_factor: Zain-ul-Abidin of Kashmir is also known for religious tolerance; Husain Shah of Bengal is associated with Hindu patronage. Ibrahim Adil Shah II of Bijapur is the one called 'Jagadguru'.
elimination_hint: Ibrahim Adil Shah II of Bijapur was called 'Jagadguru' for his secular credentials, patronage of music (Kitab-i-Nauras), and tolerance of all religions.
factual_hooks: Ibrahim Adil Shah II: Sultan of Bijapur, titled 'Jagadguru' and 'Abla Baba'
Known for Kitab-i-Nauras — treatise on music and poetry showing Hindu-Muslim cultural synthesis
contrast_with: Aurangzeb — Mughal orthodoxy vs Ibrahim Adil Shah II — Deccan syncretism

subject: Polity
trap_factor: Statement D is the trap: Dadra and Nagar Haveli was under Portuguese rule, not French. Pondicherry (not Daman/Diu) was under French rule. Daman and Diu were separated by 56th Amendment — true.
elimination_hint: Dadra and Nagar Haveli was under Portuguese (not French) colonial rule until 1954. This makes D incorrect. Goa statehood 1987 (A correct), Diu in Gulf of Khambhat (B correct), 56th Amendment (C correct).
factual_hooks: Dadra and Nagar Haveli: Portuguese till 1954, then Indian territory
Pondicherry (Puducherry): French colonial territory till 1954
Goa: Portuguese till 1961, full statehood 1987
contrast_with: Portuguese territories (Goa, Daman, Diu, Dadra & NH) vs French territories (Pondicherry, Karikal, Mahe, Yanam)

subject: Geography
trap_factor: Lake Malawi (Nyasa) also borders Tanzania and Mozambique; Lake Chad borders different countries. Victoria is the correct answer for Tanzania-Uganda boundary.
elimination_hint: Lake Victoria borders Tanzania, Uganda, and Kenya — it forms the Tanzania-Uganda international boundary. Lake Malawi is Tanzania-Mozambique-Malawi. Zambezi is a river, not a lake.
factual_hooks: Lake Victoria: borders Uganda, Tanzania, Kenya — forms Tanzania-Uganda boundary
Second largest freshwater lake in the world by surface area
contrast_with: Lake Victoria (Tanzania-Uganda) vs Lake Malawi/Nyasa (Tanzania-Mozambique-Malawi)

subject: Economy
trap_factor: Students confuse constant prices with population adjustment; constant prices eliminate inflation effect, not population growth. Current vs constant price difference is about inflation, not demographics.
elimination_hint: Current price includes inflation; constant price removes inflation. So current > constant means price level (inflation) accounts for the difference. Answer: B.
factual_hooks: Per capita income at current prices includes inflation effect
Per capita income at constant prices removes inflation (uses base year prices)
Current > Constant because inflation inflates current price figures
contrast_with: Current prices (nominal) vs constant prices (real) — inflation is the difference

subject: Geography
trap_factor: Statement A sounds plausible as Ghaggar flows through Haryana; but the Indira Gandhi Canal uses Ravi and Beas waters via Harike Barrage, not Ghaggar.
elimination_hint: Indira Gandhi Canal draws water from Sutlej-Beas link at Harike Barrage — not Ghaggar. Ghaggar is an intermittent river (lost river). So statement A is false.
factual_hooks: Indira Gandhi Canal: draws from Ravi and Beas rivers via Harike Barrage
Ghaggar: seasonal river, not a source for major irrigation canals
Nizam Sagar: on Manjra river (tributary of Godavari) — correct
contrast_with: Ghaggar (seasonal, north) vs Sutlej-Beas (perennial, source of IGC)

subject: Geography
trap_factor: Map-based question on Jammu & Kashmir cities requires precise knowledge of relative positions of Baramula, Srinagar, Anantnag, Kargil — easily confused without map familiarity.
elimination_hint: Srinagar is central; Baramula is northwest, Anantnag is south, Kargil is northeast. Sequence A (Baramula, Srinagar, Anantnag, Kargil) for ABCD placement matches option C.
factual_hooks: Srinagar: capital of J&K, in Kashmir Valley
Kargil: in Ladakh region, east of Zoji La pass
Baramula: northwest Kashmir, on Jhelum river

subject: Geography
trap_factor: Statement II is the trap — not all megacities (e.g., Delhi) are seaports. Statement III is also partially wrong (Hyderabad, Bangalore are not national capitals).
elimination_hint: Statement I (>5 million) is now outdated but was correct in 2000. Statement II is false (Delhi is not a seaport). Statement III is false (Hyderabad/Bangalore are not national or state capitals at time of question). Answer: D (I and III).
factual_hooks: Indian megacities at time of question: Delhi, Mumbai, Kolkata, Chennai — not all seaports
Delhi is not a seaport — eliminates statement II
contrast_with: Megacity (>10 million now) vs metropolitan city

subject: Polity
trap_factor: Students assume state consent is required for Parliament to enact laws implementing international treaties; Article 253 explicitly overrides this requirement.
elimination_hint: Article 253 of the Constitution: Parliament can legislate for implementing international treaties without consent of any State — even on State List subjects.
factual_hooks: Article 253: Parliament can make laws for whole/part of India for implementing international treaties without state consent
Overrides federal division of powers for treaty implementation
contrast_with: Article 249 — Rajya Sabha resolution needed vs Article 253 — no state/RS consent needed for treaties
contemporary_relevance: Relevant to India implementing Paris Agreement, WTO commitments through domestic legislation.

subject: History
trap_factor: Students often think Diarchy was introduced at both Centre and provinces by GoI Act 1935; in fact, Diarchy was abolished at provinces and introduced/retained at Centre under 1935.
elimination_hint: GoI Act 1935 abolished Diarchy in provinces (replaced by Provincial Autonomy) but introduced Diarchy at the Centre. So 'Diarchy at the Centre as well as in the provinces' (option A) is false — not a feature.
factual_hooks: GoI Act 1935: Provincial Autonomy introduced, Diarchy abolished in provinces
Diarchy at the Centre was a feature of GoI Act 1935
Also introduced: Federal Court, All India Federation (never implemented), bicameral legislatures in some provinces
contrast_with: GoI Act 1919 — Diarchy in provinces vs GoI Act 1935 — Diarchy at Centre only

subject: Society
trap_factor: Korba tribe is from Chhattisgarh, not Kodagu (Coorg) in Karnataka. Kodagu (Coorg) has the Kodava community — not Korba.
elimination_hint: Korba tribe inhabits Chhattisgarh (not Kodagu/Karnataka) — makes option D incorrect pair. Buksa in Pauri-Garhwal (A), Kol in Jabalpur area (B), Munda in Chhotanagpur (C) are all correct.
factual_hooks: Korba tribe: from Chhattisgarh, not Kodagu
Munda tribe: Chhotanagpur plateau (Jharkhand)
Buksa tribe: Uttarakhand (Pauri-Garhwal region)
contrast_with: Korba (Chhattisgarh) vs Kodava/Coorg community (Karnataka)

subject: Polity
trap_factor: The most common trap is option A — students know Money Bills are special but forget they can ONLY be introduced in Lok Sabha, not either house.
elimination_hint: Article 110: Money Bill can only be introduced in Lok Sabha, NOT Rajya Sabha. This makes option A incorrect.
factual_hooks: Money Bill: introduced only in Lok Sabha (Article 109)
Speaker of Lok Sabha certifies if a bill is a Money Bill — final authority
Rajya Sabha must return within 14 days — can only recommend, not amend
President cannot return Money Bill for reconsideration
contrast_with: Money Bill vs Financial Bill — different constitutional treatment

subject: History
trap_factor: INA was formed in 1942 in Singapore by Mohan Singh, then reorganized by Subhas Chandra Bose in 1943 — students confuse 'came into existence' (1942, Singapore) with Bose's reorganization (1943).
elimination_hint: INA was first formed in Singapore in 1942 under Mohan Singh after fall of Singapore to Japan; Bose took over and reorganized it in 1943 also in Singapore (Syonan-to).
factual_hooks: INA: first formed in Singapore, 1942 under Mohan Singh
Bose reorganized INA in Singapore 1943 after Japanese transferred it to him
Battle of Imphal-Kohima was INA's major military campaign
contrast_with: INA (Singapore, external) vs Congress (India, internal resistance)

subject: Economy
trap_factor: Options A and D confuse SENSEX with all BSE companies; SENSEX tracks 30 selected (blue chip) companies only — not all listed companies.
elimination_hint: SENSEX = Sensitive Index = weighted index of 30 selected blue-chip companies on BSE. A rise means overall rise in these 30 companies' share prices, not all BSE companies.
factual_hooks: SENSEX: BSE Sensitive Index of 30 blue-chip companies
Rise in SENSEX = weighted average rise in prices of these 30 companies
NIFTY 50 = NSE equivalent of SENSEX
contrast_with: SENSEX (BSE, 30 companies) vs NIFTY (NSE, 50 companies)
contemporary_relevance: Stock market indices are key economic indicators regularly tracked in budget and monetary policy discussions.

subject: CSAT
trap_factor: Students calculate total arrangements (6! = 720) and forget to subtract properly; the complement method requires subtracting cases where Ajit and Mukherjee ARE together.
elimination_hint: Total = 6! = 720. Together: treat A+M as one unit → 5! × 2! = 240. Not together = 720 - 240 = 480. Answer: D.
factual_hooks: Complement method: arrangements where two are NOT together = total − arrangements where they ARE together
When two people must be together: treat as one unit, multiply by 2! for internal arrangement
contrast_with: 'Never together' vs 'always together' — solved by complement method

subject: History
trap_factor: Giddha is a folk dance form from Punjab; pairing it with Jabbar Patel (who is a Marathi filmmaker) is the incorrect pair — Patel directed Marathi plays, not Giddha.
elimination_hint: Jabbar Patel is associated with Marathi theatre/film (e.g., Saamna, Simhasan) — not Giddha (Punjabi folk dance). Badal Sarkar-Baki Itihas, Vishnu Das Bhave-Sita Swayamvar, Girish Karnad-Yayati are all correct.
factual_hooks: Jabbar Patel: Marathi playwright/filmmaker — not associated with Giddha
Giddha: traditional Punjabi folk dance form for women
Girish Karnad: playwright known for Yayati, Tughlaq, Hayavadana
contrast_with: Jabbar Patel (Marathi) vs Giddha (Punjabi) — regional mismatch

subject: Geography
trap_factor: Atlas Cycle (Sonepat) and BHEL (Bangalore for BEML) are commonly confused; BEML (Bharat Earth Movers) is in Bangalore, Atlas Cycle is in Sonepat.
elimination_hint: Atlas Cycle = Sonepat (D), BEML = Bangalore (A), IFFCO = Kalol (C), NALCO = Bhubaneswar (B). Answer: D (I-D, II-A, III-C, IV-B).
factual_hooks: Atlas Cycle Company: Sonepat, Haryana
BEML (Bharat Earth Movers): Bangalore
NALCO (National Aluminium): Bhubaneswar area (Damanjodi/Angul, Odisha)

subject: History
trap_factor: Options B and C seem plausible — revolutionaries from Bengal did use Tripura; but option D (anti-kingship groups + British protector) captures the internal dynamic better.
elimination_hint: Tripura had internal groups fighting against the Manikya kingdom and its British protector — which catalyzed freedom movement engagement, not just external Bengali revolutionary influence.
factual_hooks: Tripura's freedom movement involved local groups resisting both the kingdom and British
Tripura was a princely state under British paramountcy
contrast_with: Direct British provinces vs princely states under paramountcy — different freedom movement dynamics

subject: Geography
trap_factor: Pondicherry's enclaves are spread along east and west coasts — Mahe is on west coast (Kerala), others are on east coast; confusing their north-south positions on map is the main challenge.
elimination_hint: Mahe is on Kerala (west) coast, Yanam is on Andhra coast (north-east), Karaikal and Pondicherry are on Tamil Nadu coast. A-2 (Mahe), B-1 (Karaikal), C-3 (Pondicherry), D-4 (Yanam) matches option A.
factual_hooks: Pondicherry (Puducherry) has 4 districts: Pondicherry, Karaikal, Mahe, Yanam
Mahe: only district on west (Arabian Sea) coast — in Kerala
contrast_with: Puducherry (east coast) vs Mahe (west coast) — same UT but different coasts

subject: History
trap_factor: Students confuse Champaran (first Satyagraha) with Kheda (second Satyagraha) or Bardoli (later successful campaign). Dandi is the famous Salt March.
elimination_hint: Champaran Satyagraha (1917) was Gandhi's first successful Satyagraha in India — against indigo planters. This is a fundamental historical fact.
factual_hooks: Champaran Satyagraha 1917: Gandhi's first successful Satyagraha in India
Against indigo plantation system (tinkathia system)
Led to Champaran Agrarian Act and end of tinkathia
contrast_with: Champaran (1917, against planters) vs Kheda (1918, against revenue collection) — both early Gandhian Satyagrahas

subject: History
trap_factor: The assertion (military campaigns as origin) and reason (expansion during Gupta period) are both true but independently so — R does not explain why military campaigns led to feudalism.
elimination_hint: Both A and R are true but R (Gupta expansion) does not explain A (military origins of feudalism). Answer B: both true but R not explanation of A.
factual_hooks: Feudalism in India traced to land grants to military commanders (samantas)
Gupta period: extensive land grants to Brahmins and officials led to feudal relationships
Military samantas became feudal lords
contrast_with: European feudalism (contractual) vs Indian feudalism (patrimonial/ritual)

subject: History
trap_factor: Both statements are true and well-known facts; the challenge is judging if R correctly explains A. Kalinga's control of trade routes IS the reason Ashoka annexed it.
elimination_hint: A: Ashoka annexed Kalinga (true). R: Kalinga controlled land and sea routes to South India (true, and this IS the strategic reason for annexation). Answer: A.
factual_hooks: Kalinga War: 261 BCE, most devastating conquest of Ashoka
Kalinga controlled vital trade routes to South India and Southeast Asia
Led to Ashoka's conversion to Buddhism and policy of Dhamma
contrast_with: Pre-Kalinga Ashoka (military expansionist) vs post-Kalinga Ashoka (pacifist)

subject: History
trap_factor: Both A and R seem true at first glance; the distinction is that early nationalists fought for interests BEYOND the narrow groups — they were broadly liberal, not narrow in interest even if narrow in social base.
elimination_hint: A is true (narrow social base — educated middle class). R is false — they actually fought for broader national interests, not the narrow interests of just their class. Answer: C.
factual_hooks: Early INC (moderate phase): dominated by educated middle class, lawyers, and professionals
They championed constitutional methods and Indianization of services — not class interests
Social base was narrow but political demands were broadly national
contrast_with: Narrow social base vs narrow political agenda — early nationalists had the former, not the latter

subject: Ethics
factual_hooks: Conflict between duty to career and duty to family/humanity
Emotional intelligence involves managing competing obligations
contrast_with: Professional duty vs humanitarian duty — core ethical dilemma
mains_intro: This case presents a classic ethical dilemma between professional commitment (IAS interview) and humanitarian duty (injured relatives), requiring balanced decision-making.
mains_body_points: Immediate human life is paramount — first ensure emergency medical help is called (ambulance/108) and someone is arranged to stay with injured relatives
Assess severity: if life-threatening and no other help available, stay and assist — life takes precedence over career opportunity
If manageable help arranged, proceed to interview; inform panel of the circumstances — most will respect humane decision
Document the situation — compassionate ground for re-scheduling may be available
Key principle: A civil servant must demonstrate both competence and compassion — abandoning injured relatives without ensuring care would reflect poor character
mains_conclusion: The ideal response balances humanitarian duty with professional responsibility — ensuring emergency help for relatives first, then attending if possible, demonstrating exactly the character expected of an IAS officer.
mains_interlinkages: GS1: Social bonds and family obligations
contemporary_relevance: Tests whether candidates embody civil service values of compassion, integrity, and sound judgment under pressure.

subject: Economy
trap_factor: Option A (Y = C+I+G+X) omits imports; option B wrongly adds M and subtracts X. The net exports (X-M) must be added, not M added separately.
elimination_hint: Open economy GDP = C + I + G + (X - M) where X-M = net exports. Imports are subtracted because they represent foreign production. Answer: C.
factual_hooks: National Income formula: Y = C + I + G + (X - M)
X-M = Net Exports / Trade Balance
(X-M) positive = trade surplus; negative = trade deficit
contrast_with: Closed economy: Y = C + I + G vs Open economy: Y = C + I + G + (X-M)

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Students may select 'very large size' assuming larger mass traps light; it is the very high density (escape velocity exceeding speed of light) not just mass that matters.
elimination_hint: Black hole property: Escape velocity > speed of light due to extremely high density (matter packed in tiny volume). Size is small, density is enormous. Answer: C.
factual_hooks: Black hole: collapse of massive star into extremely dense point (singularity)
Escape velocity exceeds speed of light due to extreme density
Event horizon: boundary beyond which nothing escapes
contrast_with: Black hole (density-based) vs neutron star (dense but radiation possible)
contemporary_relevance: First image of black hole captured by Event Horizon Telescope (2019) brought renewed interest.

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Catalytic converter (IV) improves emission quality but doesn't improve fuel efficiency directly. Radial tyres reduce rolling resistance improving fuel economy, but question asks specifically about fuel efficiency.
elimination_hint: Streamlined body reduces aerodynamic drag; multipoint fuel injection optimizes fuel-air mixture. Catalytic converter treats exhaust (not fuel). Radial tyres help but minimally. Answer: B (II and III).
factual_hooks: Streamlined body: reduces drag → less engine work → better fuel economy
Multipoint fuel injection (MPFI): precise fuel delivery → complete combustion → fuel efficiency
Catalytic converter: reduces pollution but not directly fuel efficiency
contrast_with: Fuel efficiency (less fuel used) vs emission control (cleaner exhaust) — catalytic converter does latter

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Steps up/steps down voltage are transformer functions; a choke coil is an inductor that limits current by offering inductive reactance — it neither steps up/down voltage.
elimination_hint: Choke coil = inductor with high inductive reactance to AC → reduces current without wasting energy as heat. It doesn't transform voltage. Answer: C.
factual_hooks: Choke coil: inductor used to limit AC current using inductive reactance
Unlike a resistor, choke coil reduces current without significant power loss
Used in fluorescent tube circuits to limit starting current surge
contrast_with: Transformer (steps voltage) vs choke coil (reduces current) — different devices

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Quartz crystal is used in clocks/oscillators; barium titanate is a piezoelectric ceramic used in microphones. Laser beam is the correct technology for CD audio playback.
elimination_hint: CD player uses laser beam to read pits and lands on the disc surface; reflected laser intensity variations are converted to digital audio signal. Answer: C.
factual_hooks: CD player: uses semiconductor laser (780nm wavelength) to read disc
Pits and lands on CD surface encode binary data
Reflected light variations converted to audio signal
contrast_with: Vinyl record (needle/stylus) vs CD (laser beam) — different reading mechanisms

subject: History
factual_hooks: 19th century reform movements addressed sati, widow remarriage, child marriage, female education
Reformers: Raja Ram Mohan Roy, Ishwar Chandra Vidyasagar, Pandita Ramabai
Debates: tradition vs modernity, religious authority vs social progress
prerequisite_concepts: Social reform movements in colonial India
Status of women in 19th century India
contrast_with: Reform-led change vs revolutionary change in women's status
mains_intro: The 19th century social reform movement in India was fundamentally intertwined with the 'women's question' — a debate about the place of women in a modernizing yet tradition-bound society.
mains_body_points: Major issues: Sati (abolished 1829), child marriage, purdah system, widow remarriage prohibition, female infanticide, lack of female education
Sati abolition (1829): Raja Ram Mohan Roy's campaign → Regulation XVII by William Bentinck
Widow Remarriage: Ishwar Chandra Vidyasagar's advocacy → Hindu Widow Remarriage Act 1856
Female education: Jyotiba Phule and Savitribai Phule opened first school for girls (1848); Pandita Ramabai championed women's education
Debates: Traditionalists (Dharmasabha) vs reformers on whether reform was anti-Hindu; some nationalists opposed social reform as British interference
Women's agency: Pandita Ramabai, Tarabai Shinde wrote against patriarchal norms — early feminist voices
Inter-linkage with nationalism: Social reform was seen as necessary precondition for political independence by reformers
mains_conclusion: The 19th century women's question was not merely about legal reform but reflected a deeper struggle between colonial modernity and indigenous patriarchy, laying the foundation for modern Indian feminism.
mains_interlinkages: GS1: Art and Culture — changing status of women
contemporary_relevance: Continuity of issues like gender equality and women's safety makes this historically grounded topic perennially relevant.

subject: Polity
trap_factor: Students confuse constitutional provisions with conventions; many rules about the PM's position (must resign if loses majority) are conventions, not explicit constitutional text.
elimination_hint: The PM's obligation to resign on losing Lok Sabha majority is a constitutional convention, not written in the Constitution. The other options either have statutory basis or are explicitly provided. Answer: B.
factual_hooks: PM must resign if loses Lok Sabha majority: convention, not written law
Constitutional convention: unwritten but binding political practice
Finance Minister being from Lower House is also a convention
contrast_with: Constitutional convention (unwritten, binding) vs constitutional provision (written in Constitution)

subject: International Relations
trap_factor: Statement I contains specific dollar figures (600 million to 5 billion) — these figures are inaccurate/dated. Statement III claims 'triple by 2000' — also was not realized. Only II (agricultural exports) has some validity.
elimination_hint: Statement I has specific wrong figures (India's trade share in different units). Statement III (tripling by 2000) was not achieved. Only II (agricultural export boost from WTO) was a stated objective. Answer: D (II and III)... actually II and III based on 1995 context; revisit — in exam answer is D.
factual_hooks: WTO came into existence January 1, 1995 replacing GATT
India's agricultural exports were expected to benefit from reduced global trade barriers
India's actual trade share remained far below early optimistic projections
contrast_with: GATT (multilateral agreement) vs WTO (international organization with dispute settlement)
contemporary_relevance: WTO remains central to India's trade negotiations and agricultural subsidy debates.

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: The dramatic nature of the event (comet hitting a gas giant) is distinctive; Mars is sometimes thought as a target due to proximity, but Jupiter's gravity was the attractor.
elimination_hint: Comet Shoemaker-Levy 9 famously collided with Jupiter in July 1994 — widely watched astronomical event. Jupiter's strong gravity captured and broke apart the comet.
factual_hooks: Comet Shoemaker-Levy 9 hit Jupiter in July 1994 — first observed extraterrestrial collision
Comet broke into 21 fragments due to Jupiter's tidal forces before impact
Impacts left visible scars on Jupiter's atmosphere

subject: CSAT
trap_factor: Students confuse 'subcontrary' (can't both be false but can both be true) with 'contradictory' (can't both be true or both be false). I (All) and III (No) are contradictories — they can't both be true OR false.
elimination_hint: In classical logic: 'All' (I) and 'No' (III) are contraries — cannot both be true simultaneously but CAN both be false (if some are inquisitive). Answer: A (I and III).
factual_hooks: Contraries: 'All A are B' and 'No A are B' — cannot both be true, but can both be false
Classical square of opposition: universal affirmative vs universal negative = contrary relationship
contrast_with: Contrary (both can be false) vs Contradictory (exactly one must be true)

subject: International Relations
trap_factor: This is a 1995-context question about CWC signatories; Israel has signed but not ratified, and Iran has signed and ratified. Syria and North Korea were not signatories in 1995.
elimination_hint: USA, Russia, India, and Iran signed CWC; Israel signed but hasn't ratified. Syria joined only in 2013. Option A (USA, Russia, India, Iran, Israel) reflects 1995 scenario best.
factual_hooks: Chemical Weapons Convention (CWC) opened for signature 1993, entered into force 1997
India ratified CWC and declared/destroyed chemical weapons
Syria joined CWC in 2013 after chemical weapons use in civil war
contrast_with: CWC signatories vs non-signatories — North Korea, Egypt, South Sudan not members
contemporary_relevance: OPCW's role in Syria chemical weapons investigations remains contentious.

subject: Geography
trap_factor: Zanskar and Ladakh ranges are often transposed; from south to north in J&K: Pir Panjal → Zanskar → Ladakh → Karakoram. Students mix up the middle two.
elimination_hint: North to south order in J&K: Karakoram (northernmost) → Ladakh → Zanskar → Pir Panjal (southernmost). So 1→4 from north: Karakoram, Ladakh, Zanskar, Pir Panjal. Answer: B.
factual_hooks: J&K mountain ranges N to S: Karakoram > Ladakh > Zanskar > Pir Panjal
Karakoram contains K2 — second highest peak in world
Pir Panjal separates Kashmir Valley from Jammu plains
contrast_with: Greater Himalayas vs Trans-Himalayas (Ladakh, Karakoram ranges)

subject: Polity
trap_factor: The scenario of 'elected candidate losing deposit' seems contradictory; it's possible only when a very large number of candidates contest, diluting vote shares so even the winner gets less than 1/6th of votes.
elimination_hint: Deposit is forfeited if a candidate gets less than 1/6th of total valid votes polled. If many candidates split votes, even the winner may get below this threshold. Answer: D.
factual_hooks: Deposit forfeited if candidate gets < 1/6th of valid votes polled
Many candidates splitting votes can cause even the winner to lose deposit
RPA 1951 governs election deposits and forfeitures
contrast_with: Low polling percentage vs vote split — both can cause deposit loss

subject: History
trap_factor: Bal Gangadhar Tilak is associated with Ganapati and Shivaji festivals — not Satya Shodhak Sabha. Satya Shodhak Sabha was founded by Jyotiba Phule (not Tilak) in 1873.
elimination_hint: Satya Shodhak Sabha (Truth-Seeking Society, 1873): founded by Jyotiba Phule for lower caste rights — not associated with Tilak. Tilak founded Deccan Education Society. Answer: D (incorrect pair).
factual_hooks: Satya Shodhak Sabha: founded by Jyotiba Phule (1873) — for lower caste emancipation, not Tilak
Tilak: founded Deccan Education Society, Kesari newspaper, revived Ganapati festival
Jamnalal Bajaj: Satyagraha Ashram Wardha — correct pairing
prerequisite_concepts: Social reform organisations in colonial India
contrast_with: Tilak (orthodox Hindu nationalist) vs Phule (anti-caste reformer) — ideological contrast

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Refraction alone is a common guess (like prism splitting light). A CD doesn't just refract — it uses diffraction grating effect from its tracks plus reflection to produce rainbow colors.
elimination_hint: CD has microscopic tracks acting as diffraction grating; light reflects off the surface and diffracts into different wavelengths (colors). Answer: A (reflection + diffraction).
factual_hooks: CD tracks act as diffraction grating (1.6 micron spacing)
White light reflects and diffracts into component colors (rainbow)
Combination: reflection from surface + diffraction from track patterns
contrast_with: Prism (refraction only) vs CD (reflection + diffraction)

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Calcium alone is not the constituent; the compound calcium oxalate is the most common kidney stone — not just calcium, and not magnesium sulphate.
elimination_hint: Kidney stones (renal calculi): most commonly calcium oxalate crystals. Other types exist (uric acid, struvite) but calcium oxalate is most common. Answer: A.
factual_hooks: Kidney stones: ~80% are calcium oxalate
Oxalate from diet + excess calcium → crystal formation in kidney
Prevention: hydration, low oxalate diet
contrast_with: Kidney stones (calcium oxalate) vs gall stones (cholesterol/bilirubin)

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Hydrogen-oxygen and acetylene-oxygen explosions exist in different contexts; but mine explosions specifically involve methane (firedamp) mixing with air to form explosive mixture.
elimination_hint: Methane (CH₄) = firedamp in coal mines; when mixed with air in 5-15% concentration, it becomes explosive. This is the cause of most mine explosions. Answer: C.
factual_hooks: Methane (firedamp) + air: explosive mixture in coal mines
Methane is released from coal seams during mining
5-15% methane in air = explosive range
contrast_with: Methane (mine explosion) vs CO₂ (suffocation risk) — different mine hazards

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Tungsten is hard but brittle at room temperature; carborundum (silicon carbide) is hard but brittle. Nichrome is an alloy that is both hard AND ductile — unusual combination.
elimination_hint: Nichrome (nickel-chromium alloy): both very hard and ductile — used in heating elements. Carborundum is hard but brittle; tungsten is hard but less ductile; cast iron is brittle.
factual_hooks: Nichrome: Ni-Cr alloy, high hardness + ductility + high melting point
Used in electric heating elements (toasters, hair dryers)
Carborundum (SiC): very hard but brittle — not ductile
contrast_with: Brittle (cast iron, carborundum) vs ductile (nichrome, copper)

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Option D (absorbs all gases) sounds comprehensive but is wrong — silica gel specifically absorbs water vapor (moisture), not all gases. Option A (kills bacteria) is also tempting.
elimination_hint: Silica gel (SiO₂·xH₂O) is a desiccant — absorbs moisture/water vapor by adsorption. Prevents drug degradation from humidity. Answer: C.
factual_hooks: Silica gel: porous desiccant that adsorbs water vapor
Used to maintain low humidity in drug packaging to prevent degradation
Can be regenerated by heating to remove absorbed moisture
contrast_with: Silica gel (moisture absorber) vs activated carbon (odor/gas absorber)

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Kevlar (polyamides) is famously used in bulletproof vests, so option B is a strong distractor; however, polycarbonates are used specifically for bulletproof glass and transparent armor.
elimination_hint: Kevlar is polyamide used in bulletproof fabric/vests; polycarbonates (e.g., Lexan) are used for bulletproof transparent shields/glass. Question asks for 'material' broadly — polycarbonates (D) is correct.
factual_hooks: Polycarbonates: high impact resistance, used in bulletproof glass, shields, visors
Kevlar (polyamide): used in bulletproof vests/fabric — high tensile strength
Polycarbonate (Lexan): 250x stronger than glass
contrast_with: Polycarbonate (transparent bulletproof material) vs Kevlar polyamide (fabric-based armor)

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Cracking is often associated with rubber (vulcanization is rubber); smelting is associated with metals but students confuse which metal. Hydrogenation of edible fats is less known.
elimination_hint: Anchor: Vulcanization = Rubber (IV-A); Hydrogenation = Edible fats/vanaspati (III-D); Smelting = Copper/metals (II-C); Cracking = Petroleum (I-B). Answer: B.
factual_hooks: Cracking: breaking heavy petroleum fractions into lighter ones
Smelting: extracting metal (copper) from ore using heat
Hydrogenation: adding H₂ to unsaturated fats → vanaspati ghee
Vulcanization: adding sulfur to rubber for strength and elasticity
contrast_with: Cracking (breaks bonds) vs Hydrogenation (adds hydrogen) — opposite in direction

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: The Reason states ammonia can be liquified at ambient temperature at low pressures — this is false; ammonia requires moderate pressure (not low). This makes A false (R is wrong).
elimination_hint: Assertion A is true (ammonia in large cold storage, CFCs in domestic). Reason R is false — ammonia liquefies at moderate pressure, not low pressure. Answer: C (A true, R false).
factual_hooks: Ammonia: used in large industrial refrigeration — cheaper, efficient, but toxic
CFCs: used in domestic refrigerators — non-toxic, safe for homes (but ozone-depleting)
Ammonia liquefies at ~-33°C at 1 atm, or at ~10 atm at room temp — NOT low pressure
contrast_with: Ammonia refrigerant (industrial, toxic) vs CFC (domestic, ozone-depleting)
contemporary_relevance: Kigali Amendment to Montreal Protocol phases down HFCs — continuing relevance of refrigerant choice.
